questions.a: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, usually one or more tightly coupled containers.
A desired-state rollout for stateless pods.
A stable virtual endpoint for a set of pods.
Sensitive configuration such as passwords, tokens, or certificates.
Workloads that need stable identities and persistent storage.
They keep traffic away from pods that are not ready to serve requests.
They restart pods that are wedged or unhealthy.

questions.q: Why are resource requests and limits important?
questions.a: They influence scheduling and prevent one pod from consuming all the node resources.
It stores cluster state and acts as the source of truth for Kubernetes.

questions.q: Why do PersistentVolume claims matter for stateful apps?
questions.a: They decouple storage provisioning from the pod lifecycle.

questions.q: Why is a rolling update safer than replacing all pods at once?
questions.a: It limits downtime and lets you detect bad releases early.

questions.q: When would you choose a StatefulSet over a Deployment?
questions.a: When pod identity, stable storage, or ordered rollout matters.

questions.q: When should you use a NetworkPolicy?
questions.a: When you need to restrict which pods can talk to one another.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff of HPA?
questions.a: It improves elasticity, but scaling decisions depend on good metrics and warmup time.

questions.q: When would you write an operator instead of scripts?
questions.a: When a recurring cluster workflow needs continuous reconciliation.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with cluster-wide abstraction?
questions.a: It simplifies operations for users but adds another layer of complexity.

questions.q: Why are probes and resource limits often tuned together?
questions.a: Because slow startup, throttling, or OOM kills can all look like failures otherwise.
